roots.title: Introduction to PCA and Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
roots.summary: This segment introduces Principal Component Analysis (PCA) and its role in dimensionality reduction, highlighting its significance in exploratory data analysis (EDA) and feature generation. It addresses the challenges posed by high-dimensional data, which often has a smaller intrinsic dimension, and explains that dimensionality reduction aims to create a compact set of new features that retain essential information. The process is framed as a matrix factorization problem, focusing on identifying a lower-dimensional subspace that captures the original data's information. PCA is described as employing an orthonormal basis to derive latent factors indicating directions of maximum variance, enabling data compression by retaining significant directions while discarding less important ones. The segment outlines a procedure for factoring the original data matrix into a lower-dimensional matrix to approximate the original data. Additionally, it covers minimizing a loss function using Lagrange multipliers, emphasizing the importance of constraints and the optimization process, including eigenvalue decomposition in PCA for data reconstruction.

children.title: Introduction to PCA and Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
children.summary: This segment introduces Part 2 of Principal Component Analysis (PCA), focusing on its importance in exploratory data analysis (EDA) and feature generation. It discusses the challenges of high-dimensional data, which often has a smaller intrinsic dimension, and explains that dimensionality reduction seeks to create a compact set of new features that retain essential information from the original dataset. Dimensionality reduction is framed as a matrix factorization problem, emphasizing the identification of a lower-dimensional subspace or manifold that captures the original data's information. PCA is described as using an orthonormal basis to derive latent factors or scores that indicate directions of maximum variance, enabling data compression by retaining significant directions and discarding less important ones. The segment also outlines a procedure to factor the original data matrix into a lower-dimensional matrix, facilitating the approximation of the original data.

children.title: Introduction to PCA and Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
children.summary: This segment introduces Part 2 of Principal Component Analysis (PCA), focusing on its role in exploratory data analysis (EDA) and feature generation. It addresses the challenges posed by high-dimensional data, which often has a smaller intrinsic dimension, and explains that dimensionality reduction aims to create a compact set of new features that retain essential information from the original dataset. The discussion frames dimensionality reduction as a matrix factorization problem, emphasizing the identification of a lower-dimensional subspace, or manifold, that captures the original data's information. PCA is described as utilizing an orthonormal basis to derive latent factors or scores that indicate directions of maximum variance, allowing for data compression by retaining significant directions and discarding less important ones. The segment also outlines a procedure to factor the original data matrix into a lower-dimensional matrix, facilitating the approximation of the original data.

children.title: Introduction to PCA Part 2 and Dimensionality Reduction
children.summary: The segment introduces Part 2 of Principal Component Analysis (PCA), emphasizing its application in exploratory data analysis (EDA) and feature generation. It discusses the challenges of working with high-dimensional data, which may have a smaller intrinsic dimension. Dimensionality reduction aims to create a smaller set of new features that retain the essential information from the original data, facilitating visualization and modeling. The concept is framed as a matrix factorization problem, setting the stage for further exploration of PCA techniques.

children.title: Dimensionality Reduction and Matrix Factorization
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of reducing high-dimensional data to lower dimensions for visualization and modeling purposes. It highlights the concept that high-dimensional data may reside in a lower-dimensional subspace, often referred to as a manifold. The goal of dimensionality reduction is to identify a smaller set of features that can effectively capture the information from the original data. This process can be framed as a matrix factorization problem, where the original data is decomposed into two matrices that approximate the original data, facilitating a more manageable representation.

children.title: Principal Component Analysis and Latent Factors
children.summary: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) involves using an orthonormal basis where the rows are unit vectors, referred to as orthogonal vectors. The components derived from PCA are called latent factors or scores, which represent the directions of maximum variance in the data. The goal is to compress the data by retaining these significant directions while discarding less important ones. A procedure is being developed to factor the original data matrix X into a lower-dimensional matrix Z, which, when multiplied by a weight matrix W, will approximately recover the original data.

children.title: Loss Minimization for Principal Component Analysis
children.summary: The focus is on loss minimization to find a matrix W such that multiplying it by Z approximates the original data matrix X, aiming to minimize reconstruction loss. The squared loss is emphasized as a common theme, leading to the derivation of principal components. The covariance matrix of X is introduced, and the relationship between Z and W is established, indicating that Z is obtained by projecting X onto W. The segment also outlines the optimization process using Lagrange multipliers to minimize loss with respect to W, incorporating constraints.

children.title: Minimizing Loss with Lagrange Multipliers
children.summary: The segment discusses minimizing a loss function L(w) = -wDWT with respect to a weight vector W using Lagrange multipliers. It incorporates the constraint wwT = 1 to ensure W is a unit vector. The importance of setting the derivative to zero for optimization is emphasized, along with the implications of violating the constraint, which can lead to unbounded minimization. The mathematical formulation of the loss function and the derivation process are detailed, highlighting the connection between optimization methods and relevant mathematical principles.

children.title: Minimizing Loss with Lagrange Multipliers
children.summary: The segment covers the process of minimizing a loss function L(w) = -wDWT with respect to a weight vector W, using Lagrange multipliers to incorporate the constraint wwT = 1, ensuring W remains a unit vector. It emphasizes the importance of setting the derivative to zero for optimization and discusses the implications of violating the constraint, which can lead to unbounded minimization. The mathematical formulation of the loss function and the derivation process are presented, highlighting the connection between optimization methods and relevant mathematical principles.

children.title: Minimizing Loss with Lagrange Multipliers
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of minimizing a loss function with respect to a weight vector W, incorporating a Lagrange multiplier to address a constraint that W times W transpose equals one, ensuring W is a unit vector. The importance of setting the derivative to zero for optimization is emphasized, along with the implications of violating the constraint, which could lead to unbounded minimization. The mathematical formulation of the loss function and the introduction of the Lagrange multiplier are presented as part of the derivation.

children.title: Optimization Using Lagrange Multipliers
children.summary: The segment discusses the application of Lagrange multipliers in optimization problems, specifically focusing on minimizing a loss function with respect to the variable w. It outlines the derivation process, starting with the loss function L(w) and introducing the constraint wwT=1. The derivative of the loss function is taken with respect to w, leading to expressions involving constants and derivatives in vector calculus. The discussion emphasizes the connection between the optimization method and the mathematical principles involved.

leaf_chunk.text:

children.title: Lagrange Multiplier Optimization for w
children.summary: The segment discusses the optimization of the loss function L(w) with respect to the weight vector w, specifically minimizing the loss L(w) = -wDWT. It introduces the use of a Lagrange multiplier to incorporate the constraint wwT = 1 into the optimization problem. The modified function is presented as L(w, X) = -WEWr + X(wwT - 1). The derivative with respect to w is derived, although the detailed vector calculus involved is noted to be beyond the scope of the course.

children.title: Optimizing Loss Function with Lagrange Multiplier
children.summary: The segment discusses the process of minimizing a loss function L(w) = -wDWT using derivatives. It involves setting the derivative equal to zero and solving for w. The optimization incorporates a constraint using the Lagrange multiplier, leading to the formulation L(w, X) = -WEw + λ(wwT - 1). The derivative is taken with respect to w, resulting in the equation -2DwT + 2Aw = 0, which is a key step in the optimization process.

children.title: Principal Component Analysis: Derivation and Data Reconstruction
children.summary: The discussion covers Principal Component Analysis (PCA), explaining that principal components are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, which indicate directions of data stretching. The optimization process involves eigenvalue decomposition and Lagrange multipliers to minimize a loss function under the constraint of a unit norm. It emphasizes that the first principal component corresponds to the largest eigenvalue derived from centered data. The segment also details data reconstruction in PCA, highlighting the centering of data and using the Z matrix to approximate original data through multiplication with principal components' transpose. While all components allow for perfect reconstruction, a smaller set is typically chosen to simplify the model while retaining essential patterns. The importance of centering data before PCA is reiterated, and the process results in decorrelated dimensions for improved visualization. Retaining both principal components is crucial for data integrity.

children.title: Principal Component Analysis and Eigenvector Derivation
children.summary: The discussion covers principal component analysis (PCA), explaining that principal components are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, sigma. These eigenvectors indicate directions of data stretching, with the goal of minimizing loss by selecting the eigenvector associated with the largest eigenvalue. The segment details the optimization process using eigenvalue decomposition and Lagrange multipliers, particularly focusing on minimizing a loss function L(w) under the constraint that the norm of w equals one. It is emphasized that the first principal component is the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue, derived from the covariance matrix formed from centered data (X minus mu), and that eigenvectors are critical for PCA analysis.

children.title: Principal Components and Eigenvectors in PCA
children.summary: The discussion focuses on principal component analysis (PCA), highlighting that principal components are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, denoted as sigma. The eigenvectors represent directions along which data is stretched, and the goal is to minimize loss by selecting the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue. This process involves eigenvalue decomposition and optimization techniques, including the use of Lagrange multipliers.

children.title: Eigenvectors and Optimization of Loss Function
children.summary: The discussion focuses on eigenvectors, specifically that w is a unitary eigenvector of the covariance matrix. It emphasizes that the error is minimized when w corresponds to the eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue. The derivation involves minimizing a loss function L(w) using a Lagrange multiplier to enforce the constraint wwT = 1. The process includes taking derivatives and setting them equal to zero to find the optimal w.

children.title: Principal Component Analysis Derivation
children.summary: The process of deriving principal components involves minimizing a loss function with respect to the weight vector w. The first principal component is identified as the eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue, which minimizes the loss. The derivation uses Lagrange multipliers to impose the constraint that the norm of w equals one. The derivative of the loss function leads to the conclusion that w must be a unitary eigenvector of the covariance matrix, and the error is minimized when w is the eigenvector with the largest eigenvalue.

children.title: Eigenvectors and Covariance Matrix Derivation
children.summary: The segment discusses the computation of eigenvectors from the covariance matrix, emphasizing that the eigenvectors are derived from the centered data, represented as X minus mu. The covariance matrix is formulated as X times X transpose. It is highlighted that the eigenvectors of X transpose X are crucial for the analysis. Additionally, it is noted that the eigenvector w is unitary and minimizes error when it corresponds to the largest eigenvalue, with a reminder that w is treated as a row vector in the derivation.

children.title: Principal Components and Eigenvectors in PCA
children.summary: Principal components are defined as the eigenvectors corresponding to the largest eigenvalues of the covariance matrix, which represent the directions of maximum variance in the data. To compute the latent factors (Z matrix), the centered data is projected onto these principal component vectors. The importance of centering the data by subtracting the mean is emphasized, and the algorithm of singular value decomposition (SVD) is introduced as a method for computing the eigenvectors.

children.title: Introduction to Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
children.summary: Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is a key concept in linear algebra that allows for the decomposition of matrices into three parts, facilitating the identification of eigenvectors. While the lecture will not cover the full details or proofs of SVD's validity or its application in Principal Component Analysis (PCA), it will focus on the essentials needed for PCA. Students are encouraged to refer to external resources, such as the EE16 notes, for a more comprehensive understanding of SVD.

children.title: Introduction to Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
children.summary: Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) involves decomposing a matrix X into three matrices: U, S, and V, where U and V are orthonormal and S is a diagonal matrix. The decomposition allows for the reconstruction of the original matrix X. The shapes of the matrices are specified, with U being m by t, S being d by d, and V being d by d, where d is the rank of the matrix. The columns of U are ordered from largest to smallest singular values, and the columns of V represent the eigenvectors of XX. SVD provides a compact representation of the matrix and offers insight into its eigenvalues and rank. A demonstration using Python's NumPy library to perform SVD is also mentioned.

children.title: Data Centering and Standardization in PCA
children.summary: In PCA, the initial step involves centering the data by subtracting the mean of each attribute column to create a centered matrix. Standardization may also be applied, which involves dividing each dimension by its standard deviation to achieve a variance of one. This process is crucial because it ensures that the variance is uniform across the dataset, allowing for accurate principal component analysis. The principal components are derived using SVD, where each component is a unit vector that minimizes the sum of squared distances between data points and their projections, while remaining orthogonal to previous components.

U, S, Vt = np. linalg.svd(X, full_matrices = False)
[ ]: print ("Shape of U", U. shape)
print ("Shape of S", S. shape)
print ("Shape of Vt", Vt. shape)
S is a little different in NumPy . Since the only useful values in the diagonal matrix S are the singular values on the diagonal axis, only those values are returned and
they are stored in an array.

children.title: Running SVD with Normalization Steps
children.summary: The process of running Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) on a standardized and centered matrix X is explained. It is noted that for efficiency, the full_matrices parameter is set to false. Normalization of the data is emphasized, particularly when the units are on different scales, by dividing each column by its standard deviation. The SVD is computed using NumPy, resulting in matrices U, S, and Vt, with S being returned as an array of singular values rather than a full diagonal matrix.

Audio: Let's run SVD. So this is what it looks like to run SVD. It's pretty easy. I say SVD. To my matrix, matrix X, I have cleaned up standardized centered, so on. So, from HC's to false because I don't want a- I set full matrices to true which is the default that's going to build more than I need to compute. So it's just being more efficient easier to work with. So that's for me just to pass. And it's going to need me these two matrices. Video: 1: File

children.title: Singular Value Decomposition and Matrix Properties
children.summary: The discussion covers the breakdown of matrix X into component matrices using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). It explains that S is a diagonal matrix representing the array of diagonal values, highlighting the concept of 'almost zero' in numerical computations, which reflects precision issues in real math on computers. The rank of the matrix is identified as three. The iterative algorithm for refining vector angles to achieve maximum variance directions is introduced, emphasizing the importance of normalizing data by dividing each column by its standard deviation before performing SVD. The shapes of the resulting matrices U, S, and Vt from the SVD are also noted.

So this is what we call close to zero. So this is a rank three matrix, which we'll come back to. That's true in a second. Right, so those some of the basics that up, let's go back to the slides. children.title: Singular Value Decomposition and Covariance Matrix
children.summary: The segment discusses the relationship between Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) and the covariance matrix of a centered data matrix X. It explains that the covariance matrix is represented as X^T X and derives this using the SVD of X, expressed as X = USV^T. The process involves manipulating transposes and applying identities related to orthonormal matrices, specifically noting that U^T U equals the identity matrix. The final expression for the covariance matrix is shown as V S^2 V^T, highlighting the connection between principal components and the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.

children.title: Singular Value Decomposition and Eigenvectors
children.summary: The segment discusses the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of a matrix X, represented as X = USVT, where U and V are orthogonal matrices and S is a diagonal matrix containing singular values. It explains that because X is centered, the covariance matrix can be expressed as V = XT X, leading to the relationship EV = VS2VT. This indicates that the principal components are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, linking the concepts of SVD and eigenvector systems.

children.title: Eigenvectors and Principal Components in PCA
children.summary: Principal components are identified as the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix derived from the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of the data matrix X. The SVD is expressed as X = USV^T, and since X is centered, the covariance matrix can be computed as W = X^T X = (USV^T)^T (USV^T) = V S^2 V^T. Right multiplying by V leads to the conclusion that the columns of V represent the principal components, which are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.

children.title: Singular Value Decomposition and Principal Components
children.summary: The singular values from the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of a centered matrix X are crucial for understanding variance. The SVD decomposes X into three matrices, with the largest singular value corresponding to the first principal component, which is an eigenvector of the covariance matrix. The covariance matrix is expressed as E = X^TX, and through the SVD, it can be shown that the columns of matrix V are the eigenvectors of this covariance matrix, thus representing the principal components.

children.title: Singular Value Decomposition and PCA
children.summary: The segment discusses Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) as a method for matrix decomposition into three components: U, S, and V. It explains that the columns of U are ordered eigenvectors corresponding to the largest singular values, while V contains orthonormal columns. The first k columns of V represent the first k principal components, allowing for the construction of a latent vector representation of the matrix X by projecting it onto these principal components. Additionally, the covariance matrix is defined as x minus mu transpose times x minus mu.

children.title: Principal Components and Covariance Matrix
children.summary: The mean of the centered data is zero, leading to the covariance matrix represented as 1/n * X^T X, where X is decomposed using Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) as X = USV^T. The covariance matrix can be computed as V^T S^T U^T U S V^T. Principal components are identified as the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.

children.title: Computing Z from SVD and PCA
children.summary: The segment discusses two methods for computing the latent vector representation Z from the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of matrix X, expressed as X = USV^T. The first method involves multiplying X by the matrix of eigenvectors V, while the second method utilizes the first k columns of matrices U and S. It emphasizes that the first k columns of V represent the principal components, and thus, Z can be computed by projecting X onto these components.

children.title: From SVD to PCA: Latent Vector Computation
children.summary: The singular value decomposition (SVD) of matrix X is expressed as X = USVT, where the first k columns of V represent the first k principal components. By projecting X onto these principal components, we can compute the latent vector representation Z, given by Z = XV = US. A demonstration is provided for constructing a 2 principal component approximation (k=2), illustrating how to derive the latent vectors using the specified components.

children.title: Constructing Principal Component Approximation
children.summary: A single-valuity composition is constructed using a 4-dimensional dataset, projecting it into 2 dimensions using the first two principal components from matrix v. The process involves multiplying the data matrix x by the principal component matrix v to obtain a new matrix for plotting. The discussion includes steps for visualizing the data in two dimensions and references the function mp for further computations.

children.title: Understanding Matrix Rank and Singular Values
children.summary: To determine if a matrix has a rank less than its dimensions, one can use singular value decomposition (SVD) and examine the singular values. If any singular values are close to zero, the matrix is not full rank. In a 4-dimensional dataset, the rank can be 3 if one singular value is zero. Numerical rounding errors can affect the last singular value, making it appear nearly zero. Additionally, the contribution of each singular value to the total variance indicates that most variance is captured by the first two dimensions, aligning with the properties of rectangles.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So if you want to know if something is almost zero, you can say it's close to zero, and this measures up to the precision of your floating point numbers. Round it up remember the diagonals because it's a 4-dimensional data set but we discussed this in last lecture. I have linked the perimeter. Area and height. is a function of length and height of a rectangle, so it's technically a rank 3 data set, you know, to have 4 dimensions. And if I look at the singular values, it tells me one of the dimensions has rank has a zero singular value, which means it is rank 3. You might have asked in previous lectures how could I figure out the rank of a matrix? Here's how. Take the singular value decomposition and look at those singular values. Right? There we go to zero or very close to zero. Then you have a matrix that is not for rake. Video: 1: File

leaf_chunk.audio: So if you want to know if something is almost zero, you can say it's close to zero, and this measures up to the precision of your floating point numbers. Round it up remember the diagonals because it's a 4-dimensional data set but we discussed this in last lecture. I have linked the perimeter. Area and height. is a function of length and height of a rectangle, so it's technically a rank 3 data set, you know, to have 4 dimensions. And if I look at the singular values, it tells me one of the dimensions has rank has a zero singular value, which means it is rank 3. You might have asked in previous lectures how could I figure out the rank of a matrix? Here's how. Take the singular value decomposition and look at those singular values. Right? There we go to zero or very close to zero. Then you have a matrix that is not for rake.

children.title: Matrix Reconstruction and Variance Analysis
children.summary: The discussion focuses on reconstructing a rank 3 matrix with four columns, emphasizing the use of singular values and total variance. The reconstruction process involves multiplying matrix X by the transpose of matrix V, leading to a scatter plot of the data. It is noted that the data should be standardized before reconstruction. Numerical rounding errors are acknowledged, indicating that the last singular value is effectively zero. The contribution to total variance is computed, revealing that most variance is captured in the first two dimensions, which aligns with the geometric properties of rectangles.

children.title: Data Reconstruction and PCA Overview
children.summary: The segment discusses data reconstruction using Principal Component Analysis (PCA), highlighting the process of centering the data and utilizing the Z matrix to approximate the original data X through multiplication with the transpose of principal components (VT). It emphasizes that while all principal components allow for perfect reconstruction, a smaller set (K) is typically chosen to simplify the model while capturing essential patterns. The importance of centering the data before applying PCA is reiterated. The PCA process involves centering the data matrix to adjust the mean to the origin, followed by rotating the data to align the direction of maximum variance with the x-axis. This transformation results in decorrelated dimensions, improving data visualization by projecting onto two dimensions. Retaining both principal components is essential to preserve data integrity.

children.title: Data Reconstruction and PCA Overview
children.summary: The discussion focuses on data reconstruction using Principal Component Analysis (PCA). It explains that by centering the data and utilizing the constructed Z matrix, one can approximate the original data X by multiplying Z with the transpose of the matrix of principal components (VT). While using all principal components allows for perfect reconstruction of the data, in practice, a smaller set of components (K) is chosen to simplify the model and reduce information while still capturing essential patterns. The importance of centering the data before applying PCA is also emphasized.

children.title: PCA Data Transformation Process
children.summary: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) begins by centering the data matrix, which involves adjusting the data points so that their mean is at the origin. Following this, PCA rotates the data to align the direction of maximum variance with the x-axis, effectively transforming the data into a new coordinate system where the first principal component captures the most variation.

children.title: PCA Data Transformation and Visualization
children.summary: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) involves centering the data matrix and rotating it to align the direction with the most variation along the x-axis. The first principal component captures maximum variance, while the second captures the remaining variance, resulting in decorrelated dimensions. This transformation allows for a clearer visualization of data by projecting it onto two dimensions. Retaining both principal components preserves data integrity, while using only one would lead to loss of information.

children.title: PCA Data Transformation Process
children.summary: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) involves centering the data matrix and then rotating it so that the direction with the highest variation aligns with the x-axis. This rotation helps in visualizing the data spread along the principal components.

children.title: Normalizing Data and Principal Component Analysis
children.summary: Data normalization is crucial for Principal Component Analysis (PCA). The data must be centered at the origin to ensure that the covariance matrix is accurately represented. PCA involves transforming the data matrix by first centering it and then rotating it to align the direction of maximum variance with the x-axis. Principal components are orthogonal to each other and are axis-aligned when plotted in a 2D space. This process allows for the extraction of latent factors by projecting the original data onto the principal components.

When running PCA it is important to center the data (ensure data is centered at 0):
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
X = X - np.mean(X, axis = 0)
· The Scikit-Learn PCA code does this automatically
You should also consider standardizing your data (ensure unit variance on each dimension)

The Scikit-Learn PCA code does not do this
You should standardize your data if features have vastly different ranges
You should not standardize your data if the units are all the same and the differences in
variability across dimensions is meaningful (e.g ., everything is in the same units)

children.title: Centring and Standardizing Data for PCA
children.summary: When performing PCA, it is crucial to center the data so that it is centered at zero, which can be done using the formula X = X - np.mean(X, axis = 0). This step is automatically handled by the Scikit-Learn PCA implementation. Additionally, standardizing the data to ensure unit variance across dimensions is recommended, particularly when features have vastly different ranges, using the formula X = (X - np.mean(X, axis = 0)) / np.std(X, axis = 0). However, if all features are in the same units and the variability differences are meaningful, standardization may not be necessary.

children.title: Standardizing Data for PCA Analysis
children.summary: Standardizing data is crucial when performing Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to ensure that each dimension contributes equally, especially when they are measured in different units. The process involves centering the data by subtracting the mean and then standardizing it by dividing by the standard deviation. This helps to prevent one dimension with a larger range from dominating the analysis. While standardization is generally recommended, there are cases where it may be rational to leave the data unstandardized if the variability is meaningful and consistent across dimensions. The Scikit-Learn PCA implementation automatically centers the data.

Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
X = X - np.mean(X, axis = 0)
·
The Scikit-Learn PCA code does this automatically
You should also consider standardizing your data (ensure unit variance on each dimension)
X = (X - np.mean(X, axis = 0)) / np.std(X, axis = 0)
The Scikit-Learn PCA code does not do this
You should standardize your data if features have vastly different ranges
You should not standardize your data if the units are all the same and the differences in
variability across dimensions is meaningful (e.g ., everything is in the same units)

children.title: PCA Data Transformation and Visualization
children.summary: Software packages do not automatically perform PCA, so manual analysis is required. A PCA model is created using two principal components, with data fitting and transformation demonstrated through code. The PCA process involves centering the data matrix and rotating it to align the direction of maximum variation with the x-axis. Visualization of the PCA results is shown using RIGIL data, noting that sign flips do not affect the overall interpretation. A comparison to the scikit-learn PCA implementation is also mentioned.

children.title: Principal Component Analysis and Covariance
children.summary: The segment discusses the implementation of Principal Component Analysis (PCA), emphasizing that the covariance matrix of the transformed data should have zero off-diagonal elements. It explains that the variability is aligned with the axes, and a plot is shown where blue dots represent the original data dimensions (width, height, area of rectangles) and red dots represent the first two principal components. The relationship between width, height, and area is noted to be curved due to their multiplicative nature. The segment concludes with a demonstration of the covariance of the transformed data being diagonalized.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: No, this is the transform PCA. Let's skip to the commands. Well, I guess we can look at it. So what I run PCI, one of the important things that happens is my covariance matrix on my transform data should be zero off diagonal. So I've made the covariance of each of the dimensions, my projected dimensions. The covariance across the mention should be zero. So this is zero, zero, but the variability will just be along the tag. That's taking that foot-borne flipping it so it's aligned with each of the axes. Let's plot it. This is what you see. children.title: PCA Data Reconstruction and Approximation
children.summary: PCA (Principal Component Analysis) involves reconstructing data from a lower-dimensional representation, which can lead to information loss. The discussion highlights how the best approximation is achieved using a two-dimensional subspace, emphasizing the process of reconstructing data points and the limitations of dimensionality reduction in preserving original data fidelity.

I moved off of where it is, so I've approximated that point. But this is the best approximation I could construct, given a plan representation. So that's 11 to 2 dimensional subspace. children.title: Data Reconstruction and Standardization Process
children.summary: The data reconstruction process involves two main steps following standardization. First, the standardized data is unstandardized by multiplying it by the original data's standard deviation and then adding back the mean. This two-step process is crucial for accurately reverting the data to its original coordinate system.

children.title: Data Reconstruction and Standardization Process
children.summary: Reconstruction of data involves two main steps after standardization. First, the standardized data must be unstandardized by multiplying by the original data's standard deviation and then adding back the mean. This process shifts the data points back to their original position. The discussion emphasizes the importance of understanding this two-step reconstruction process to accurately revert the data to its original coordinate system.

children.title: Total Variance and Principal Component Analysis
children.summary: Total variance in a data matrix is defined as the sum of the variances of its attributes, exemplified with specific values leading to a total variance calculation of 402.56. The segment covers reconstructing data using latent factors from Singular Value Decomposition (SVD), detailing steps to scale factors back to the original coordinate system and visualize the data with a 3D scatter plot. It emphasizes maximizing total variance in data reconstruction, explaining the significance of singular values in SVD, where diagonal entries of the S matrix represent component scores indicating variance explained by each principal component. The actual variance is obtained by dividing each singular value by the number of observations, with squared singular values corresponding to the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix. The discussion also includes the variance calculation in principal component analysis (PCA), noting that the first component captures the most variance, followed by subsequent components with progressively less importance, suggesting that components with lower variance may be less significant and potentially discarded.

children.title: Total Variance in Data Matrix Analysis
children.summary: Total variance of a data matrix is defined as the sum of the variances of its attributes. An example is provided with specific values for width, length, and area, leading to a total variance calculation of 402.56. The segment also discusses reconstructing data using latent factors derived from Singular Value Decomposition (SVD), detailing the steps to construct and scale the factors back to the original coordinate system, followed by visualizing the data with a 3D scatter plot.

## Reconstruct the original rectangle using the factors Z and the principle components
rectangle_hat = pd. DataFrame(Z @ Vt [ : k, : ], columns = rectangle. columns)
## Scale and shift the factors back to the original coordinate system
rectangle_hat = rectangle_hat * np.std ( rectangle, axis = 0) + np.mean(rectangle, axis = 0)
## Plot the data
fig = px. scatter_3d(rectangle, x="width", y="height", z="area",
width=800, height=600)
fig. add_scatter3d(x=rectangle_hat ["width"],
y=rectangle_hat ["height"],
z=rectangle_hat ["area"],
mode="markers", name = "approximation")

children.title: Capturing Total Variance and Singular Values
children.summary: The discussion focuses on maximizing total variance in data reconstruction, emphasizing the importance of singular values in the SVD composition. It explains that the diagonal entries of the S matrix represent component scores that indicate the variance explained by each principal component. To obtain the actual variance, each singular value must be divided by n, where n is the number of observations. The squared singular values correspond to the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix, highlighting their role in variance measurement.

children.title: Variance in Principal Component Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses the calculation of variance in principal component analysis (PCA). It explains how the total variance of a data matrix is the sum of the variances of its attributes. The speaker details the variance associated with each principal component, highlighting that the first component captures the most variance, followed by the second and third components with progressively less variance. This allocation of variance indicates which dimensions are most important for reconstructing the original data, suggesting that components with less variance may be less significant and could potentially be discarded.

children.title: Variance Ratios and Principal Components Analysis
children.summary: The segment discusses the calculation of variance for principal components using singular values. It explains how to derive the variance ratio for each principal component, indicating how much total variance is explained by each dimension. The focus is on understanding the importance of these ratios in assessing the overall variance captured by the principal components. Additionally, it mentions the construction of PCA plots to visually evaluate the data projected onto the first two principal components.

children.title: Calculating Variance Ratios in PCA
children.summary: The segment discusses how to compute the variance ratios for principal components in Principal Component Analysis (PCA). It explains that the variance for a specific component is calculated as the square of its singular value divided by the total variance, which is the sum of the squares of all singular values. The formula presented is s² / sum(s²). Additionally, it notes that if the first two singular values are significantly larger than the others, two dimensions may suffice to capture most of the distinguishing features of the data. The segment also includes a poll asking participants how to compute variance ratios, reinforcing the importance of understanding this concept.

children.title: Scree Plot and Variance Ratios in PCA
children.summary: The concept of a scree plot is introduced as a tool for visualizing the importance of each dimension in principal component analysis (PCA). The term 'scree' originates from geology, referring to a slope of loose stones. The scree plot displays variance ratios of principal components, illustrating how much each component contributes to the total variance. The variance ratio for each principal component is calculated using the formula: component score j divided by the total variance. The segment also discusses how to compute these variance ratios using NumPy's linear algebra functions, specifically the SVD (Singular Value Decomposition) method.

children.title: Understanding Scree Plots and PCA Dimensions
children.summary: Scree plots illustrate the variance ratio captured by each principal component, highlighting that if the first two singular values are large while the others are small, two dimensions suffice to represent the data effectively. It is noted that real data sets often show rapid decay in eigenvalues, indicating that most significant information is contained in the first few dimensions. A discussion on interpreting these plots will follow, and a YouTube video resource on Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is recommended for further understanding.

children.title: Resources on Singular Value Decomposition and PCA
children.summary: Steve Brunton's video series on Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is recommended as a great resource. The videos cover foundational concepts, applications, and related statistical topics, emphasizing the importance of linear algebra. However, it is noted that his definition of Principal Components is incorrect.

children.title: Principal Components and Singular Value Decomposition Properties
children.summary: A video resource by Steve Brunton is recommended for further understanding of principal components, with a note on a minor error in his definition. Following a brief break, the discussion focuses on the matrices U, S, and V obtained from Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). The matrix U is identified as an N by D matrix of left singular vectors, with key properties including that U times U transpose equals the identity matrix and U transpose times U equals the identity matrix in dimensions N and D.

children.title: Orthonormal Vectors and SVD Explanation
children.summary: Orthonormal vectors are used to describe the rotation of data through orthonormal matrices, which do not scale dimensions. The eigenvectors of the matrix X transpose X are explored, demonstrating the relationship between these eigenvectors and the singular value decomposition (SVD). The columns of the matrix U, referred to as left singular vectors, are orthonormal and satisfy the conditions UUT = I and UTU = I. The process of multiplying by U and the implications for the eigenvectors of XXT are also discussed, emphasizing the algebraic transformations involved.

children.title: Understanding Left Singular Vectors in SVD
children.summary: The columns of matrix U in Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) are orthonormal and referred to as left singular vectors. The properties of U include UUT = I and U' U = I, indicating that U can be viewed as a rotation. The relationship with eigenvectors of the matrix XXT is also established, showing that XXT can be expressed as USVT, leading to further simplifications involving U and S.

children.title: Understanding Diagonal Matrices and Singular Values
children.summary: The segment discusses diagonal matrices and their singular values, emphasizing that the largest singular value appears first and that values decrease subsequently. It mentions that for a ranked efficient matrix of rank R, there are R non-zero singular values, while most values in the matrix are zero. The diagonal matrix can be used for scaling data, as it affects the magnitude of each dimension. Additionally, it touches on matrix inversion through decomposition, where singular values can be flipped.

children.title: Matrix Inversion and Singular Value Decomposition
children.summary: The segment discusses the construction of the inverse of a non-full rank matrix using the Moore-Penrose inverse, also known as the SVD (Singular Value Decomposition). It explains that the singular values are ordered from greatest to least and that the majority of the matrix consists of zeros. The singular values are non-negative and sorted, with the rank of the matrix denoted as r, which corresponds to the number of non-zero singular values. The matrix S can be interpreted as a scaling operation.

children.title: Understanding Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
children.summary: The segment discusses the components of Singular Value Decomposition (SVD), emphasizing the significance of matrices S and V. S contains singular values ordered from greatest to least, representing scaling operations, while V consists of right singular vectors that are orthonormal. The relationship between the matrices is explored, including the equation X^T X = V S^T S V^T, highlighting the role of V in rotation and its connection to eigenvectors. The importance of these concepts in principal component analysis is also mentioned.

children.title: PCA via SVD and Variance Ratios
children.summary: The singular value decomposition (SVD) of matrix X is given by X = USVT, where the first k columns of V represent the first k principal components. The latent vector representation of X can be constructed by projecting it onto these principal components, resulting in Z = XV = USVTV. To compute variance ratios for principal components, the formula s**2 / sum(s**2) indicates that if the first two singular values are large while others are small, two dimensions suffice to capture most distinguishing features of the data. A poll is conducted to assess the understanding of how to calculate these variance ratios.

Congressional Vote Records
Let's examine how the House of Representatives (of the 116th Congress, 1st session) voted in the month of September 2019.
From the U.S. Senate website:
Roll call votes occur when a representative or senator votes "yea" or "nay," so that the names of members voting on each side are recorded. A voice vote
is a vote in which those in favor or against a measure say "yea" or "nay," respectively, without the names or tallies of members voting on each side being
recorded.
-
The data, compiled from ProPublica source, is a "skinny" table of data where each record is a single vote by a member across any roll call in the 116th Congress, 1st
session, as downloaded in February 2020. The member ofthe House, whom we'll call legislator, is denoted by their bioguide alphanumeric ID in
http://bioguide.congress.gov/.
[ ]:
# February 2019 House of Representatives roll call votes
#
Downloaded using https://github.com/eyeseast/propublica-congress
votes = pd. read_csv('data/votes.csv' )
votes = votes. astype( {"roll call": str})
votes
Suppose we pivot this table to group each legislator and their voting pattern across every (roll call) vote in this month. We mark 1 if the legislator voted Yes (yea), and
0 otherwise (No/nay, no vote, speaker, etc.).
[ ]: def was_yes (s) :
return 1 if s. iloc [0] == "Yes" else 0
vote_pivot = votes.pivot_table( index='member',
columns='roll call'.

children.title: Analyzing Congressional Vote Data and Patterns
children.summary: The segment discusses the analysis of congressional vote data, focusing on roll call votes from the House of Representatives in September 2019. It describes the data set structure, including member IDs and their votes across 41 bills, involving 441 members. The challenge of visualizing this 41-dimensional data set is addressed, with pivot tables introduced as a method to rearrange and understand voting patterns. Data is centered and standardized for analysis, creating a pivot table that marks votes as 1 for 'Yes' and 0 for 'No' or other options. The resulting pivot table consists of 441 rows and 41 columns, representing each legislator's voting behavior. The process includes calculating U, S, and V transpose matrices, where S is diagonal, and V contains the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, facilitating data visualization.

children.title: Analyzing Congressional Vote Data
children.summary: The segment discusses the analysis of congressional vote data, focusing on roll call votes from the House of Representatives during September 2019. It describes the structure of the data set, which includes member IDs and their votes (yes or no) across 41 bills, totaling 441 members. The challenge of visualizing this 41-dimensional data set is highlighted, and the use of pivot tables is introduced as a method to rearrange and make sense of the voting patterns.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: we're going to look at congressional votes to get the data here. This is the raw data. I have each row is a roll call, which is like one of these votes that the house is voting on. The house member number, remember ID and what they voted. Sometimes it invotes, sometimes it vote yes, sometimes it vote no. How do I make sense of data like this? This is really hard to visualize. We're going to focus on the Yester's and the Nose. In a pivoted table and you can go through this this is a cool use of pivot tables. I've rearranged it so the roll call these are the bills they're voting on. And there's 41 of them. And there's 441 members of the house and this is their member number. And this is whether or not they voted yes or no. This is a 44, sorry 41 dimensional data set. How do you visualize this? How do we make sense of how people vote? So, OPCA is brilliant. Video: 1: File

Congressional Vote Records
Let's examine how the House of Representatives (of the 116th Congress, 1st session) voted in the month of September 2019.
From the U.S. Senate website:
Roll call votes occur when a representative or senator votes "yea" or "nay," so that the names of members voting on each side are recorded. A voice vote
is a vote in which those in favor or against a measure say "yea" or "nay," respectively, without the names or tallies of members voting on each side being
recorded.
-
The data, compiled from ProPublica source, is a "skinny" table of data where each record is a single vote by a member across any roll call in the 116th Congress, 1st
session, as downloaded in February 2020. The member ofthe House, whom we'll call legislator, is denoted by their bioguide alphanumeric ID in
http://bioguide.congress.gov/.

children.title: Data Centering and Voting Pattern Analysis
children.summary: Data is centered and standardized for analysis of House representatives' voting patterns, which involves creating a pivot table that marks votes as 1 for 'Yes' and 0 for 'No' or other options. The resulting pivot table has 441 rows and 41 columns, representing each legislator's voting behavior across various roll calls. The process includes calculating U, S, and V transpose matrices, where S is diagonal, and V contains the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix, enabling visualization of the data in a plot.

Component Scores
If the first two singular values are large and all others are small, then two dimensions are enough to describe most of what distinguishes one observation from another.
If not, then a PCA scatter plot is omitting lots of information.
An equivalent way to evaluate this is to determine the variance ratios, i.e ., the fraction of the variance each PC contributes to total variance.

If the first two singular values are large and all others are small, then two dimensions are enough to describe most of what distinguishes one observation from another.
If not, then a PCA scatter plot is omitting lots of information.
An equivalent way to evaluate this is to determine the variance ratios, i.e ., the fraction of the variance each PC contributes to total variance.

If the first two singular values are large and all others are small, then two dimensions are enough to describe most of what distinguishes one observation from another.

An equivalent way to evaluate this is to determine the variance ratios, i.e ., the fraction of the variance each PC contributes to total variance.

children.title: Principal Component Analysis of Voting Data
children.summary: The segment discusses the initial step in Principal Component Analysis (PCA) applied to voting data, emphasizing data dimension reduction to enhance understanding of voting behavior. It explains that the first principal component captures the majority of variance in voting patterns, indicating that a significant portion of behavior can be represented in just two dimensions, which account for approximately 85% of the data variance. A third dimension contributes an additional 2% variance, and visualizations in both two and three dimensions are provided to illustrate these insights.

children.title: Principal Component Analysis of Voting Data
children.summary: The segment discusses the first step in Principal Component Analysis (PCA), focusing on reducing data dimensions to better understand voting behavior. It highlights how the first principal component captures most of the variance in voting patterns, suggesting that a significant portion of voting behavior can be described using just two dimensions, which account for about 85% of the data. A third dimension adds an additional 2% variance, and visualizations in two and three dimensions are presented to illustrate these findings.

Component Scores
If the first two singular values are large and all others are small, then two dimensions are enough to describe most of what distinguishes one observation from another.
If not, then a PCA scatter plot is omitting lots of information.
An equivalent way to evaluate this is to determine the variance ratios, i.e ., the fraction of the variance each PC contributes to total variance.

children.title: Visualizing Political Data Clusters
children.summary: The discussion focuses on visualizing political data by plotting member attributes such as political affiliation, gender, state, and age. Different shapes represent gender, colors indicate political party affiliation, and dot sizes correspond to individuals' ages. This visualization reveals two distinct clusters in the data, raising questions about their potential meanings.

children.title: Visualizing Political Data Clusters
children.summary: The discussion centers on visualizing political data by plotting various member attributes, including political affiliation, gender, state, and age. Different shapes represent gender, while color indicates political party affiliation. The size of the dots corresponds to the age of the individuals, allowing for a visual representation of how these factors cluster together. The speaker notes the emergence of two distinct clusters in the data, prompting a question about what these clusters might represent.

Green is the independent, the independent. Red conservative. Now I've made the size correspond to the age I think, right? What do you hear? I made size, corresponding to age. So little dots are young. Big dots are old. I also attach her name so we can start to explore how people live in this space. Turns out it's kind of a problem. children.title: Principal Component Analysis in Political Data
children.summary: The segment covers the application of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) in political data analysis, illustrating a one-dimensional political spectrum from liberal to conservative based on voting patterns. It explains that the first principal component indicates political affiliation, while a second dimension reveals additional factors affecting political identity. Visual representations of data points are used to demonstrate these concepts, addressing issues like overplotting and suggesting jitter for clarity. The discussion also includes the use of PCA in exploratory data analysis (EDA) to explore relationships among political figures, emphasizing character independence and encouraging further investigation. Additionally, the segment highlights the role of PCA in visualizing high-dimensional data by reducing it to two or three dimensions, identifying clusters and structures, and understanding relationships among various attributes.

children.title: Principal Component Analysis in Political Data
children.summary: The segment discusses how Principal Component Analysis (PCA) can reveal underlying structures in political data, specifically highlighting a one-dimensional political spectrum from liberal to conservative based on voting patterns on bills. The first principal component represents political affiliation, while a second dimension suggests additional factors influencing political identity. The speaker demonstrates the concept using a visual representation of data points, noting issues with overplotting and suggesting the use of jitter to improve clarity.

children.title: Exploring Political Character Independence with EDA
children.summary: The segment discusses the use of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) in exploratory data analysis (EDA) to understand the relationships between political figures, specifically mentioning Nancy Pelosi and others. It highlights the importance of examining character independence and how PCA can reveal underlying patterns in political data, encouraging further investigation into the data presented.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: I don't know what that is. I kinda wanna understand it, right? This is where PCI is pretty cool, so now I can come down here and I go where are these? Characters. Independence. Elijah Cummings, James, Clive Bern. I can't see who that is. The NC Pelosi. What is she doing down there but she was extreme liberal should she be over here. Stooding differently to some other dimension which she seems to care about. This is a tool in EDA. I don't actually have the answers these things. I would then want to dig in. All right, so Nancy Pelosi is a special division. Video: 1: File

children.title: Visualizing High-Dimensional Data with PCA
children.summary: The discussion focuses on using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to visualize high-dimensional data, such as voting patterns, by reducing it to two or three dimensions. The speaker emphasizes the importance of identifying clusters and structures within the data, particularly on different axes, and how these visualizations can reveal insights about the relationships between various attributes. The segment encourages exploration of the data to understand the separations and groupings present in the lower-dimensional space.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: In fact, all of these people are special. They differed on this other axis. Where's this other principle component? can look at that person component think about it in terms of each of the the bills. So, see you in a second. Just like this in Hard Dimensions. There's a more structure here. This third dimension, the Z-axis, I don't see as much information. But I might want to know, like on this axis, there's people way up here and down here. What is it that seems to separate them on this third dimension? What collection or cluster of bills is contributing to that? It's a really cool way to visualize my data. So again, maybe a high point is you're going to take high-dimensional data. like voting that's hard to visualize will apply PCA and you'll be able to put it in two or three dimensions you can play with it. But that's sort of the beginning. Again, you're going to often attach other information attributes and try to see if there's structure and how those attributes lay out in that lower dimensional space. Now let you reveal something about your data. Do you mind when I explore further? Clusters of who's nearby each other, who's not? A little bit further at this because I had a lot of fun. So again, maybe a high point is you're going to take high-dimensional data. like voting that's hard to visualize will apply PCA and you'll be able to put it in two or three dimensions you can play with it. But that's sort of the beginning. Again, you're going to often attach other information attributes and try to see if there's structure and how those attributes lay out in that lower dimensional space. Now let you reveal something about your data. Do you mind when I explore further? Clusters of who's nearby each other, who's not? A little bit further at this because I had a lot of fun.

children.title: Voting Patterns and Abstention Analysis
children.summary: The discussion covers the analysis of voting behavior, noting that not all individuals participate in every vote and some only engage on personally significant issues. It emphasizes the importance of vote frequency, suggesting that infrequent voters may exhibit different patterns. The speaker acknowledges a prior mistake in treating abstentions as negative votes, prompting a reassessment of their categorization. Additionally, the concept of principal components in voting data analysis is introduced, leading to the creation of a pivot table that includes only Yes/No votes while excluding non-voting records.

children.title: Analysis of Voting Patterns and Abstention
children.summary: The discussion focuses on analyzing voting behavior, highlighting that not all individuals participate in every vote, with some voting only on issues of personal significance. The speaker emphasizes the importance of considering the frequency of votes cast, suggesting that individuals who vote less frequently may represent a different voting pattern. An initial mistake in treating abstentions as negative votes is acknowledged, indicating a need to reassess how abstentions are categorized. The segment also introduces the concept of principal components in the analysis of voting data, leading to the creation of a pivot table that only includes Yes/No votes while excluding non-voting records.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: You can look at votes, not everyone votes. Some people just like, send up for Congress and then check out, whether old and couldn't participate, maybe sick. So you can look at the faction of voting individuals. I think I prioritize people voted more often. That you're here. Yes, size and revotes so we can see like people down here just didn't vote that often. So they're kind of weird probably because they only voted on that one bill that matter to them and everything else was no. I made a mistake maybe early on because I considered everything as one yes and then if you didn't vote or you said no I said no. Right, so abstention was treated probably incorrectly. So it would seem that perhaps this axis here really is to do more of abstention. An independence, perhaps they abstain from more bills than they needed to. This person has voted 41 times. He voted fairly often. on are no pattern aligns with these people down here. We'll talk about the principal components themselves. So we can plot wild plot of each of these principal components. Visual representations illustrate the breakdown of votes by party lines, highlighting average votes for each party on various bills. children.title: Voting Patterns by Party Affiliation
children.summary: Analysis of voting behavior reveals that Democrats generally support Bill 517 while Republicans oppose it. The discussion includes visual representations of votes broken down by party lines, indicating the average votes for each party on various bills. Additionally, the eigenvector analysis is used to explore the relationship between party affiliation and voting patterns, with party labels incorporated into the analysis for further insights.

children.title: Principal Component Analysis in Voting Patterns
children.summary: The discussion centers on the first principal component in voting patterns, which corresponds closely with Republican voting behavior, indicating that independent voters leaning Republican are predominant in this component. Abstention is considered a 'no' vote, affecting the analysis. The algorithm identifies this structure based solely on data and voting patterns, without prior knowledge of political affiliations. A bi-plot is introduced as a visualization method to illustrate the relationships, emphasizing the alignment of voting behavior across various party affiliations.

children.title: Principal Component Analysis in Voting Patterns
children.summary: The discussion focuses on the first principal component in voting patterns, which aligns closely with Republican voting behavior. It suggests that independent voters, particularly those leaning Republican, dominate this component. Abstention is treated as a 'no' vote, influencing the analysis. The algorithm, based solely on data and voting patterns, identifies this structure without prior knowledge of political affiliations. A bi-plot is mentioned as a method to visualize these relationships, highlighting the alignment of voting behavior across different party affiliations.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: What do you think these principal components line up with the first principal component? We just sort of inverse each other so we can expect proportions. If you look at this first principle component... It's essentially how much you align with Republican voting pattern. Are that probably dominated because independence? Just didn't really go to know for a lot of things. Hey. So, independent publicans sort of together dominated this behavior voting now. We also treat the abstention as a no, so there's another reason even a Democrat that wasn't present would vote no. To our dominant principle component, very much aligns with the average voting pattern of a Republican. So really it was how Republican are you? You have a high value in your first Facebook, Facebook, but it you're very conservative. Hello, Daya? liberal in how you vote. But again, the algorithm knew nothing about this, so just had the data. and the voting patterns. I'm able to figure out this structure in the voting data. children.title: Principal Components and Biplot Visualization
children.summary: The discussion covers principal components derived from a matrix, where each row represents a bill and its contribution to the principal components. Vectors are created for each bill based on their values in the first two principal components, enabling a visual representation in a plot. This plot illustrates the direction of each bill relative to the principal component axes, showing their alignment with the main components. A code snippet is provided to demonstrate how to create a biplot using these vectors.

children.title: Principal Components and Biplot Visualization
children.summary: The discussion focuses on principal components derived from a matrix, where each row represents a bill and its contribution to the principal components. Vectors are created for each bill based on their values in the first two principal components, allowing for a visual representation in a plot. The plot shows the direction of each bill in relation to the principal component axes, indicating alignment with the main components. The code snippet provided illustrates how to create a biplot using these vectors.

And it's how much that bill positively voting positively or negatively contributes that component. Which means I can plot... a vector for each one of these entries so this is a vector for bill one a vector for bill two and so on. Because it's two numbers, right? So I have the number one, number one, principle point of value one, principle point of value two. I'll do one here. So principle point one, principle point two, the corresponding value. Those I can plot like this in my plot. And they show me for each bill which direction it's pointing. Is that Bill Moore lining with the main, the first prince, point, Axis, the second or the combination of the two? Let's go back to our code. children.title: Creating Biplots in Python with Plotly Express
children.summary: The segment covers the creation of a biplot using Python's Plotly Express library, focusing on calculating principal components (pc1 and pc2) from a dataset. It explains how to visualize these components in a scatter plot, differentiating data points by political party and gender. Additional hover data for attributes such as name and state is included, along with configuration details for the plot title, dimensions, and color mapping for different parties.

children.title: Data Visualization in Python with Biplot
children.summary: The segment discusses how to create a biplot using Python's Plotly Express library. It involves calculating principal components (pc1 and pc2) from a dataset and visualizing them in a scatter plot. The plot differentiates data points by political party and gender, with additional hover data for individual attributes like name and state. The configuration includes setting the plot title, dimensions, and color mapping for different parties.

children.title: High-Dimensional Data Visualization and PCA
children.summary: The discussion covers high-dimensional data visualization using a fashion dataset, where images represent features. It introduces Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for visualizing 5,000 images, reshaping them into 784-dimensional vectors for analysis. PCA is applied to reduce dimensionality, extracting the first 50 principal components and indicating that the data likely exists in a 10-dimensional space due to 10 clothing classes. The segment emphasizes visualizing the first three principal components, demonstrating how to create plots to visualize explained variance and relationships between classes.

children.title: High-Dimensional Data Visualization with Fashion Dataset
children.summary: The discussion focuses on high-dimensional data, particularly using images as examples where each pixel represents a feature. A famous dataset in machine learning, containing labeled images of clothing items like dresses and t-shirts, is introduced. The objective is to explore the structure of the data rather than perform classification.

Visualizing images
The following snippet of code visualizes the images
[ ]: def show_images (images, ncols=5, max_images=30):
# conver the subset of images into a n, 28, 28 matrix for facet visualization
img_mat = np. array(images. head(max_images) ['images ' ] .to_list ( ) )
fig = px. imshow (img_mat, color_continuous_scale='gray',
facet_col = 0, facet_col_wrap=ncols,
height = 220*int(np.ceil(len(images)/ncols)))
fig. update_layout (coloraxis_showscale=False)
# Extract the facet number and convert it back to the class label.
fig. for_each_annotation(lambda a: a. update(text=images. iloc[int(a. text. split("=") [-1] ) ] ['class' ] ) )
return fig
show images ( images. head (20) )

Fashion-MNIST dataset
We will be using the Fashion-MNIST dataset, which is a cool little dataset with gray scale 28x28 images of articles of clothing.
Fashion-MNIST: a Novel Image Dataset for Benchmarking Machine Learning Algorithms. Han Xiao, Kashif Rasul, Roland Vollgraf. arXiv:1708.07747
https://github.com/zalandoresearch/fashion-mnist

Visualizing images
The following snippet of code visualizes the images
[ ]: def show_images (images, ncols=5, max_images=30):
# conver the subset of images into a n, 28, 28 matrix for facet visualization
img_mat = np. array(images. head(max_images) ['images ' ] .to_list ( ) )
fig = px. imshow (img_mat, color_continuous_scale='gray',
facet_col = 0, facet_col_wrap=ncols,
height = 220*int(np.ceil(len(images)/ncols)))
fig. update_layout (coloraxis_showscale=False)
# Extract the facet number and convert it back to the class label.
fig. for_each_annotation(lambda a: a. update(text=images. iloc[int(a. text. split("=") [-1] ) ] ['class' ] ) )
return fig
show images ( images. head (20) )

[ ]: def show_images (images, ncols=5, max_images=30):
# conver the subset of images into a n, 28, 28 matrix for facet visualization
img_mat = np. array(images. head(max_images) ['images ' ] .to_list ( ) )
fig = px. imshow (img_mat, color_continuous_scale='gray',
facet_col = 0, facet_col_wrap=ncols,
height = 220*int(np.ceil(len(images)/ncols)))
fig. update_layout (coloraxis_showscale=False)
# Extract the facet number and convert it back to the class label.
fig. for_each_annotation(lambda a: a. update(text=images. iloc[int(a. text. split("=") [-1] ) ] ['class' ] ) )
return fig

children.title: Introduction to PCA and Image Representation
children.summary: The segment introduces Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for visualizing a dataset of 5,000 images, each 28x28 pixels. It emphasizes the need to understand the high-dimensional representation of the images by extracting the image matrix from the dataframe. The images are then reshaped into a single row vector of 784 dimensions to facilitate PCA analysis.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Does it pull over? Is it more like a shirt or a plow? T-shirt. You probably know what that is, but maybe I didn't. Maybe I don't understand Tasha. I understand how this space looks. Where are these points lit? and look at each class. I post this code so you can play with the code and try different samples of it. Alright, let's do PCA. So we have... 5,000 by 28 by 28. My images, so 5,000 images, they're 28 pixels by 28 pixels. Video: 1: File

children.title: PCA for Image Dimensionality Reduction
children.summary: The segment discusses using Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to reduce the dimensionality of image data from a dataset containing clothing types. It explains the process of reshaping the pixel data into a 784-dimensional vector and centering the data by subtracting the mean. PCA is then applied to extract the first 50 principal components, with a note on the explained variance ratios indicating that the data likely exists in a 10-dimensional space due to the presence of 10 clothing classes. The speaker also mentions the potential similarity between certain classes, like t-shirts and pullovers.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Black and white so just how bright is that pixel. Re-shape that into 5,784. D is big, 784. Strike off the mean. standardizing again because these pixels are in the rough pixel magnifus space. I don't need to standardize. What units are about the same? I'm gonna run PCI and take the first 50 principal components. I'll probably just use one or two, maybe three, but I had 15 in case I wanted to look more. I get the explain variance ratios that's built into the PCA object save some time here We'll spy by 100. percentage. We can see here there's a little more structure. Here's some data in first, second, third, fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh, all we have to 10. Interesting. It goes to about 10. Why would it go to about 10? Important detail, there are 10 classes, 10 types of clothing here. So in some sense, this data really lives in something close to a 10-dimensional space, but I'd argue t-shirts and pullovers are pretty similar. Video: 1: File

children.title: PCA Visualization and Data Explanation
children.summary: The discussion focuses on visualizing the first three principal components extracted from the data, particularly emphasizing that most of the data variance is captured in the first two or three dimensions. Code snippets demonstrate how to create line plots and scatter plots to visualize the explained variance and the transformed data in two and three dimensions, highlighting the relationship between different classes in the dataset.

children.title: PCA for Data Visualization in Fashion Data
children.summary: The segment discusses the application of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for visualizing high-dimensional data, specifically in the context of fashion items like trousers, coats, and shoes. It highlights how PCA helps to identify structure within the data by reducing dimensions, allowing for clearer visualization of clusters, such as the grouping of jeans. The speaker emphasizes the importance of using PCA not only for visualization but also for simplifying data in various modeling techniques.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: Now we can see the different types of clothing, so trousers or adjacent to coats, interesting dresses. Dresses over here. Coats, T-shirts, and then what's over here? We've got sneakers, ankle boots, sandals, pullover, that's weird. Shoes and stuff, proper in this dimension. So now to see some structure in this data. Good hard dimensions. See the more structure. So I need three, maybe four or five dimensions to explain this data. to like, what are brighter images or darker images and see if it breaks down differently. But there is pretty clear structure, like ankle boots seem to live up here, sneakers, and shoes on this side of the axis, the side of the plot. I can start to see hey these jeans seem to cluster in how they are expressed in different parts of my plot. Now I might want to study these groups of jeans together. Right. All right, so this is how we use PCA for for data visualization. It is how we take high-dimensional data like images, votes and try to make sense of them in lower dimensions for visualization. And again, for some modeling techniques you might do this too, you might apply PCA to simplify your data. Video: 1: File

children.title: Overview of PCA and Upcoming Topics
children.summary: PCA projects data onto a matrix W and principal components, with an exercise for students regarding the limited impact of additional matrix multiplication. The next lecture will focus on clustering, followed by a Thanksgiving break. Post-break, the course will cover neural networks and large language models, including a detailed explanation of ChatGPT's functionality. The final two lectures are expected to be engaging.

children.title: Overview of PCA and Upcoming Topics
children.summary: PCA projects data onto a matrix W and principal components, with a note that additional matrix multiplication may have limited impact, which is an exercise for students. The next lecture will cover clustering, followed by a Thanksgiving break. After the break, the course will address neural networks and large language models, including a detailed explanation of how ChatGPT functions, aiming to clarify its complexities. The final two lectures are anticipated to be engaging.

children.title: Overview of PCA and Upcoming Topics
children.summary: PCA projects data onto a matrix W and principal components, suggesting that additional matrix multiplication may have limited impact, which is left as an exercise. Next week's lecture will focus on clustering, followed by a Thanksgiving break. After the break, the course will cover neural networks and large language models, including a detailed explanation of how ChatGPT works, making it less of a mystery. The last two lectures are expected to be engaging.

leaf_chunk.text:
Audio: So remember PCA is going to just project my data onto a matrix W and just some onto my principal components. And all my linear models are also projecting my data onto matrix. So Doing an additional matrix multiply may not do much. You can think about why that is. Leave that for an exercise. Alright. That is it for PCA. Starting next week, we have just one lecture and then we have Thanksgiving break. Next week's lecture is on clustering, which is another really fun algorithm for how we break our data into different groups. And then you get a break and we come back and you should all come to lecture. We're going to do neural nets for the first time and then we'll do large language models and I will explain how a chat to BT works in all of its detail. And so after that lecture, it will no longer be magic. So the last two lectures should be a lot of fun. Have a great Thanksgiving. I look forward to seeing a few of you on Tuesday and then hopefully the rest of you. The week after. Bye. Video: 1: File
